chunks.text: Okay, we ready to go? Hello. Phoebe, is it? Yes. Hi, I'm here. Hello. Hi, Phoebe. I'm Dana. I'm the chair of the subcommittee. And we are here to hear from you about the Disability Support Services Bill, of course. Mm-hmm. You have five minutes with us, and you're welcome to use that as you see fit- Right. Thank you... when you go through your submission.
Sometimes it's good to leave a question, but we'll leave that up to you to decide. So I'll just start the timer and you can- Yep... get underway. Groovy. Thank you.

So kia ora koutou. I'm Phoebe Eden-Mann. I'm the national policy analyst for CCS Disability Action. Thank you for the opportunity to speak to you guys about this today. We do support the need for a strong legislative foundation for the disability support services. However, we are deeply concerned that this bill in its current form will cause avoidable and measurable harm to disabled people and their whanau.

Our primary recommendation is that the Select Committee require the government to rewrite the bill in full, following meaningful consultation with disabled people, families, whanau, and representative organizations. Our first concern is the lack of consultation. The bill got introduced with very limited to no meaningful consultation, despite the fact that it directly affects disabled people's lives, independence, supports, and rights.

This falls short of our obligations under the United Nations Convention on the Rights of Persons with Disabilities, particularly the requirement to closely consult with and actively involve disabled people in matters that affect them. The consultation period's also been too short, particularly of a bill of this scale and for communities that rely on accessible information and take some time to absorb it and process it, and then provide their views.

Our second concern is Clause 8 for the proposed families first principle. The bill places responsibility onto families and whanau and other culturally recognized family groups before public support is provided. This also defines family extremely broadly, extending well beyond immediate family. We believe that this is unreasonable.

Disabled people should not have to exhaust their family tree before receiving basic disability support. Family and whanau support is really important and it's deeply valued, but it cannot replace publicly funded support. Without clear limits, this bill risks making families responsible for lifelong care, regardless of their capacity, well-being, health, or financial situation. That's not sustainable, and it increases the risk of isolation, poverty, burnout, and unsafe care arrangements. It also undermines disabled people's rights to live

independently and to be included in the community, which they're guaranteed under Article 19 of the rights of the convention. We're especially concerned about the impact on women, older carers, and families who are already under pressure. Evidence cited in our submission shows that most family and whanau carers are women. Many are older.

Many experience physical and financial relationship strain because of caring. Disabled people and their families are also more likely to experience poverty. Asking already stretched families to provide more before publicly available support is available is both unrealistic and harmful. So in summary, CCS Disability Action recommends that the bill be rewritten after meaningful consultation with disabled people and their whanau. If the bill proceeds, we recommend drastically narrowing the definition of family responsibility, setting clear

limits of what families are reasonably expected to provide, requiring assessments for carer wellbeing and sustainability to provide, removing provisions to allow potential means testing, and ensure any future disability support programs uphold the rights of disabled people.

This bill affects the independence, dignity and safety and wellbeing of disabled people across Aotearoa. Nothing about us without us only works if the voices of disabled people are present, and this bill has not only failed to seek out those voices but will have negative impact for thousands of disabled people and their loved ones.

We urge the committee to ensure that disabled people's voices are central and that any future law strengthens rather than weakens their rights and supports. Thank you, and I'm happy to take any questions if you have any. All right. Thank you. Questions? Anyone? I just have a point of clarification- Yes... Madam Chair.

Maureen. Thanks very much for your submission, Chloe. I just- Phoebe. Phoebe, sorry. My apologies. You mentioned the issue around using family support first. Mm-hmm. Just if you wish to get the copy of the minister's first reading speech- Mm-hmm... she clarifies quite a few of the points that have been raised in your submission. And using the family support first is something that is already in practice in terms of things like spectacles and hearing aids maybe, or modifications to the house, ramps, et cetera.

Yeah. So to your point at the beginning, this is really just putting in place legislation that reflects existing practice. So I just wanted to give you some reassurance there. It's- Our concern is that this bill in its present form- Yeah... with the big list of all the family members that you're expected to go through- Mm-hmm... which includes cousins, stepkids, all that- Yep... that is an unreasonably broad list, and that's not how it currently is at the moment.

Yeah. And I take your point, the wording could be a lot clearer. Mm. And we've had that feedback from- Yeah. So that's- That's definitely- That's our main concern is that as it is currently from the bill, taken directly from the bill-... that wording is c- cause of huge alarm in the community. And that's not the community's fault.

That's taken directly from their own government's legislation. Yeah. And we've heard that feedback. So that's our concern. Great. I think that's- We'll get that up... very clear, Phoebe. Thank you. Thank you. And appreciate your submission. Thanks for taking the time to come and talk to us. Thank you very much. Bye. Thank you. We will move on to Complex Care Group submission.

Do we have Sheree? We heard from Sheree the other day, I think. Is she online? Yeah. She's online. Is it Lisa Martin? Yeah. Oh, Lisa Martin. Right. I'll get Sheree from-- Oh, Sheree's on here. Oh, prepared by Sheree and Lisa. And both. Right.

Lisa, are you with us? Can you hear us? Kia ora. Yes, I am. Oh, kia ora. Hello, how are you today? Good, good, thank you. Excellent. Nice to see you. If you would like to go through your submission, you've got five minutes with us, and we'll maybe if there's some time for some questions at the end.

But otherwise, over to you how you use your time. Thank you. My name is Lisa Martin. I'm the director of Complex Care Group Trust, and I'm pleased to have the opportunity to share the insights and the expertise of our organization with the Select Committee for the Disability Support Services Bill. Complex Care Group is a disability information advisory service predominantly assisting family members of those with very high and complex disability needs.

And this is largely due to the nature of complex disabilities, where the disabled person often does not have the ability to communicate their own preferences, and for this, they depend instead on those who deeply know and understand them, which is usually close family. Our main concerns about the Disability Support Services Bill, family to hold responsibility for the care of the disabled person in the first instance.

Families already turn to family first. From infancy through childhood, it is typical to call upon family first to take up kind offers of support from family and friends. This becomes exponentially more difficult as the profoundly disabled child grows older. To insist on family first responsibility could put families at risk from physical injuries and psychological trauma, especially if it involves extended family who do not regularly provide such support.

The type of care required often involves lifting, transferring, use of hoists, tube feeding, oxygen use, et cetera. Care of those with high and complex needs represents significant time, patience, and expertise in exactly which supports are required when and how, and why. Extended family will likely lack training and expertise in the correct methods of providing care and behavior management, et cetera.

Families are already exhausted. We know of some families who literally provide support around the clock, and whilst there may be support workers involved for some care needs, parents still contribute extraordinarily high levels of support. It is already family who fills the gap when support workers are unwell or taking leave.

Risk of abuse, either from exhausted parents to the disabled person or the disabled person to the family member. The loss of choice and control around who can be a disabled person's support worker could lead to immense frustration and distress for the disabled person. Child to parent violence and abuse is a very real thing.

Families are often dispersed across the motu or the globe. There is no guidance in the bill as to how stringently family first will be imposed. Family first does not seem practical in 2026 New Zealand when family members will have their own careers, families, and commitments of their own. The remarkably detailed list of what comprises family is worrying. There is no guidance in the bill regarding which circumstances might require not only parents, but siblings, aunts, uncles, and even first cousins to become caregivers. Requiring family first

involvement does risk fracturing family relationships. Means testing in the same way the detailed list of family could be interpreted as meaning that extended family members may be means or asset tested for their disabled family member. Means testing preferably needs to be removed from the bill or significantly clarified. It is said that means testing already exists only for two supports.

Equipment and modification services and household management requires a community services card, and means or income testing is certainly not the only eligibility requirement for those supports. Means testing or income testing is likely to produce unintended consequences. Money is likely to be spent or protected in a trust to avoid scrutiny. And how can it be equitable for a family who has assets that are not protected in a trust to be required to pay?

The intended elimination of paid family carers, who are commonly parents, is problematic. The choice to become a paid family carer is not always a choice. It's often driven by the lack of suitably trained or experienced staff. Constant staff turnover drives many parents to provide the care themselves to ensure continuity and less stress for the disabled person. Removing this option for carers to be paid will require them to turn to other paid work, and a greater number of support workers will be required.

Where will they come from? Exactly who provides support should be a choice. Paid family members, or family being employed by a provider organization, or the employment of external staff. These are the choices that need to be retained so disabled people and families can select the best option for them.

We request the bill be abolished and a fresh approach involving consultation prior to the writing of a disability support services bill is undertaken. Family carers have huge wisdom to share. Thank you. Thank you very much, Lisa. Very good submission, and I note your recommendations in the back of your submission, which we will take note of.

Sadly, we've run out of time for questions, but we will definitely look at your recommendations, which were very clear. Thank you. Thank you. Thanks for your time. Thank you. Okay. Next is Parent/Family/Whānau Network. Kia ora.

Rebecca. Yes, Dr Rebecca Graham. All right. Please, yes, go ahead with your submission. You've got five minutes. You can see the dreaded clock up there. Tēnā koutou katoa. I am Rebecca Graham, and I am the chair of the Parent/Family/Whānau Network. The network is a collective of nationally relevant grassroot organizations that variously represent the shared lived experience of parents and whānau within the disability sector.

We have extensive lived experience and sector knowledge of the realities of caring and being family within a disability context. Network members would have been more than happy to share our expertise and knowledge with the minister in developing this bill. We were never asked. We did not even know about this bill until it was before Parliament.

Disabled people have generously expended their time, energy, and resource over this week to share their knowledge and expertise. I'm immensely proud to be part of such a generous, caring, and diverse sector. Diversity means we often disagree, yet this bill has united us. Disabled people, families, advocates, carers, organizations, services, and providers have all found this bill to be deeply flawed. When the whole sector is united, it is sensible to listen.

The minister tells us that this bill sets the legislative foundations for disability support services. Foundations determine what gets built next. This legislation is not just about what happens now or today, but about what it will allow to happen tomorrow. As currently written, the bill does not provide a solid, safe, and secure foundation for disability supports and allows far too much uncertainty for what might happen tomorrow.

As noted by others, Clause 8 does not allow disabled people to have choice and control over their lives, including over who provides their care. Part 1 of the bill defines family extremely broadly, and this broad definition creates an ever-increasing circle of responsibility for families.

The bill provides no safeguards or limits when it comes to expectations on family care. There is no age of majority in this bill, and there is no age at which family is no longer considered responsible for their disabled family member. There is no limit in this bill on the care expected by family and no government responsibility towards families.

Families have asked to partner with government, receive adequate payment and recognition for their care work, and to have choice and control over the care they provide. This bill does not currently provide for this adequately. Worryingly, the bill sets the foundations for burnout, harm, and overwhelm for families.

It legislates via Clause 8 into law the very conditions that lead to abuse and neglect. The recent findings from the Royal Commission of Inquiry into Abuse and State Care were clear. Disabled people must have their rights and protections embedded into legislation. Human rights obligations, including the UNCRPD, must be embedded into law, and the law must guarantee disabled individuals the right to choose their care arrangements.

Disability support services must be appropriately monitored, regulated, and funded to ensure that disability supports are safe and abuse-free. These recommendations by the Royal Commission are not currently and fully upheld in the current bill. Foundational disability legislation must set the groundwork for abuse-free, neglect-free lives.

It must be done in partnership with disabled people in their communities, and it must uphold and center the rights of disabled people and families. We know from our history that we cannot blindly trust the government, but that our rights must be upheld in legislation. We need this bill to uphold rights and protections, not expose us all to potential harm.

We recommend, along with the previous submitters, starting again in consultation with disabled people and their families. Ga-mai. Oh, thank you very much for your submission. Do we have any questions of the submitter? Sorry. Maureen's having a small moment.

Look, I think it's pretty clear what you have to say. I guess my question was, I noted you talked about partnering with government, and choice and control of the outcomes is what people want. What does that look like for you? In terms of being a family member, choice and control means that when we are talking about funding and going through the NASC process and talking about what works for our family, is being able to say, "Actually, I don't want to be a paid carer for my child, but what would work for us and our family is being able to employ

someone else." Or, "Actually, what would work for us is to be able to have some informal care arrangements where we can provide a koha so we're not draining family, but we're not necessarily entering into that formal employment contract." And it means having that trust base with the person that you're having those conversations with, your NASC or your EGL, and being able to work out what's going to work best for the whole family.

Mm. And yes, family provides care. I think we all agree that that happens. But the way it's currently legislated is really worrying because of what it allows and the lack of safeguards for family. And I think it's really important that legislation has safeguards and really important that legislation doesn't inadvertently create conditions that can be misinterpreted or abused.

Okay. That's a good answer. Thank you very much. Thank you. Thank you for your time and thank you for coming in today. All right. Te Tohu o Whakaha Mihini. The Anglican Disability Network. I think they will not serve today. Yeah. Come back to them? Mm-hmm. Okay. We'll go on now to the FASD CAN Incorporated, Ross Porter. Ross, yeah.

Are they online? Right. Got some worries. Mm. All right. Who do we have, sorry? We've got Ross Porter here. Ross Porter. FASD CAN Incorporated. I'm sure he will help me understand what that is, stands for. Kia ora koutou. My name is Ross Porter.

I'm a board member of FASD CAN Incorporated. It's a national charitable organization representing people living with fetal alcohol spectrum disorder and families and whanau who support them. Ross, we can't see you and that's fine if that's how you want it to be. Oh, I'm sorry. I- Just letting you know if you were- Understood.

... wondering why we're not looking at you. I'm just finding where to click. There we go. It doesn't improve much when I turn the video on, I'm afraid to say. Okay. I think just carry on. We don't seem to be able to. Yeah. Oh. Oh, there we go. That looks more hopeful. There we are. Happy days. Right.

Shall we just start again, shall we? Given that you... One moment. Right. Away you go, Ross. My name's Ross Porter. I'm a board member of FASD CAN Incorporated. It's a national charitable organization representing people living with fetal alcohol spectrum disorder and families and whanau who support them.

FASD is a lifelong disability. People living with it typically have profound challenges with memory, decision-making, impulse control, and understanding consequences. Many need support every day, not just as children, but throughout their adult lives. There is no cure for FASD. Right now, only around one in five people we represent qualify for disability support services because of how eligibility is currently defined. The other 80% are left without funded support, relying almost entirely on their families.

That's already an injustice, and this bill, as drafted, risks making it permanent and worse. Our most serious concern is Clause 11, the power given to the minister alone to determine who is eligible for disability support. To explain why this matter, I just want to tell you about a typical family situation.

Their adult son, I'll call him James, has FASD along with an intellectual disability. His IQ has been assessed at 68, so he does qualify for disability support services. Qualifying on paper and actually receiving reliable support has turned out to be two very different things. James is 26, can't manage money, can't hold down a job.

He cannot live safely alone. His mother has been his carer since he was born. She's now in her late 50s. She has her own health problems, and she has almost no savings because she gave up her career to take up part-time work, which allows her to care for him. Even with James meeting the eligibility criteria, his mother has spent years fighting the system just to get the level of support he's entitled to.

His funding has been reassessed and reduced twice. This is what happens to a family today under a system that already involves a measure of administrative discretion. Clause seven, allowing the minister to redefine who qualifies and on what terms without parliamentary scrutiny and democracy, not requiring consultation with the Disability Community Panel- It could be changed in time.

It's creating a lot of uncertainty for those families, and probably fear of what might happen. Clause eight, the family first principle, means that whenever support is reduced or delayed, James' mother will be expected to fill the gap with no assessment of what that costs her in terms of her health or her financial position. We're not asking the committee to oppose the bill. We accept that a legislative framework is needed following the Fleming judgment.

What we are asking is that parliament, not a minister acting alone, retains meaningful control over who receives disability support. I've got some recommendations here, but hopefully you've got my submission in front of you, and you'll just- Yes... take them from that. Yes, we do. So in closing, the families we represent are not asking to be relieved of their love or commitment. They are asking to be treated honestly and fairly, to have some certainty that they will not be asked to carry an unlimited burden without support, without acknowledgment, and without any recourse to when they

reach their limit. James' mother deserves better than that, and so does James. So do the families who don't even have what little certainty he has. Incidentally, James has a friend who also has a diagnosis of FASD and lives with many of the same challenges as James, i.e., deficits in a number of his brain domains, but he has an IQ of 75. Therefore, he isn't currently eligible for DSS at all.

I'd just like to say, ngā mihi, thanks for listening, and if you've got any questions, I'll see if I can answer them. Yes. Dr David Wilson. Yeah. Thank you, Ross. Could you give us an idea of some of the reasons that- Chair... people with FASD are declined? Next. Simply because they don't have an IQ below- 75 ... 70, actually.

And in fact, if you click on the NASC's website, you'll see some of them avoid the grief of having to deal with us by saying, "Please don't apply if you have FASD," without an intellectual disability. Yep, I can see your concern. It's incredible, I would have to say.

Look, thank you very much, Ross. I think you make yourself very clear, and we will take into account your recommendations when we are deliberating later on and looking at the bill itself. So thank you for your time, and well communicated to us. Thank you. Thanks. Thank you very much. Ngā mihi.
Excellent. So we will move now to the Hastings District Council Disability Reference Group. We get Eloise.
Eloise, can you hear us?

There we go. Kia ora. Kia ora. Can you hear us? Yes, we can hear you, and we can see you. Thank you very much. You have five minutes with us. I'm Dana. I'm the chair of the committee. We have your submission in front of us, but we're very pleased to hear from you, so please go ahead.

And if there's time for questions at the end, we will ask those if we're within time. Fantastic. Thank you, Dana. My name is Nick Ratcliffe. I'm a Hastings district counselor. I chair the disability advisory group here. I'm actually going to hand over to one of our members, Debbie Ward. We've got about six members in the room here with us.

Ah. I'm going to hand over to Debbie to present our oral submission. Kia ora. Kia ora koutou katoa. Kia ora. Ko Debbie Ward toku ingoa, and it's my pleasure today to represent the voice of the Hastings District Council's disability advisory group, of whom I'm a member as a resident of Hastings.

Really, in our submission, we've put that we would like for this bill to be paused and undertake further development rather than progressing it as it is. We'd like to see some co-design of the legislation with the disability community to ensure that it reflects the real experience and needs of the disabled people and their whanau. To build in clear safeguards and accountability mechanisms, including transparency and review of rights.

And clarify the key elements up front around eligibility, supports, and processes instead of deferring them to later decisions. We'd like to see improvement in the consultation practices, ensuring accessibility, time, and support so that disabled people can meaningfully participate in their communities.

We believe that the bill-- So the question you asked was: What changes do we want to see to the bill? It should be redeveloped in a genuine partnership with disabled people, whanau, carers, providers, and representative organizations. This legislation must clearly define eligibility, access to supports, safeguards, reviews, rights, and transparent decision-making processes.

The role of natural supports should not replace funded supports or place unreasonable pressure on families. And there should be adequate consultation time frames, accessible information, and proper support to enable meaningful participation. Many disabled people and whanau have indicated that they would have loved to have had an opportunity to put a submission in, but the time frame was just not enough for them to do it in a way that was meaningful for them.

What is our biggest concerns? There are several major concerns that we have with the bill. Over-reliance on natural supports, potentially shifting responsibility onto families and carers. Lack of detail in the bill, with too much left to future regulations and policy designs.

In essence, the biggest concern is that the bill creates significant risk to access, fairness, and accountability in disability support services due to weak design and poor consultation. We would love to be able to answer any questions. Thank you very much. It was a very good submission.

Do we have questions from the committee? Maureen Pugh. No. No? Oh. No, but I do thank you for your recommendations and your submission. And I would also refer submitters, as I have done before, to the minister, Louise Upston's first reading speech in the House, which gives a lot more context to the bill that we have in front of us.

But I can assure you that the intention for the remainder of the secondary legislation will be done in consultation. This is providing the platform. And so business as usual. This is not going to affect any existing entitlements or service to the disabled community.

But I do appreciate that some of the wording could be improved so that that misunderstanding is cleared up. And I think there's a lot of anxiety out there in the community. Mm-hmm. Around this has created a lot of fear, anxiety, and unnecessary stress, and I think that wording should be corrected immediately and become inaccessible in alternate formats where everyone can understand it.

Yes, I agree. Mm. Yeah. Thank you, Maureen. My understanding is that, as you say, the existing situation isn't meant to change in terms of funding. In fact, we've funded more, but clearly the wording in the legislation doesn't reflect that, so it's created this situation. So thank you very much for your great submission.

Kia ora to all of your colleagues in the room with you from here in Wellington and we thank you very much for your time today. Thank you. Tena koutou. Kia ora. Okay. Where are we up to, team? Neuroscience. Neuroscience and Trauma-Informed Network Aotearoa.
Online? Mm-hmm. Yes. Perfect.
All right.

Okay. Do we have Frian, Friane with us? Yes. Kia ora. Oh, kia ora. How are you? I'm Dana. Good. Nice to meet you. I'm the chair of the subcommittee here to hear you today. So you have five minutes. Please use that time as you see fit. Kia ora. Tena koutou katoa.

Thank you first of all for this opportunity to present orally. Ko Frian Waria toku ingoa. I am first and foremost a parent of three young adults with disabilities and neurodivergence, and the founder and chairperson for NATINA, which is the Neuroscience and Trauma-Informed Network Aotearoa.

I'm also an early intervention specialist, an advocate in the community, and I'm a huge promoter for systemic change that upholds inclusion, disability rights, and well-being for our disabled children, adults, and the families. NATINA is a charitable trust established by myself and other community members in response to the trauma, discrimination which we have experienced as families, members of disabled people because of the ongoing discrimination, systemic barriers, inadequate supports, and resources.

Our work is grounded in the belief that every person deserves to live with dignity, belonging, and equitable opportunities to thrive. So to answer your questions, what changes do we want to see? We want to see the bill ensure that disabled people over the age of 18 will be supported by the government of Aotearoa, New Zealand in a way that guarantees access to the disability supports and services they need to live a good, meaningful life and fully realize their rights and potential.

We also ask that the bill removes the expectation that family members will provide ongoing care and support for disabled adults. For many families, this is simply not sustainable. This is not because families lack love, commitment, or care for their disabled person. Rather, many have already endured years of hardship, discrimination, inequity, inadequate supports, financial pressure, systemic barriers that have impacted their own health and well-being.

Expecting families to carry lifelong caring responsibilities without adequate supports is neither fair nor realistic. What is our biggest concern? Our greatest concern is that the bill creates uncertainty about the future eligibility and access to disability supports By insisting on natural supports first and allowing significant decisions to be made by ministers without sufficient safeguards or meaningful consultation with disabled people and their whānau.

In short, it increases the risk for trauma and inequity for disabled people and their families. What is our proposed solution? So we ask the committee to ensure that all future decisions, amendments, considerations, implementation of this bill are guided by trauma-informed principles. And the principles I'm going to go by one by one.

This means upholding safety by ensuring disabled people and their whānau are heard, valued, and supported. It means demonstrating trustworthiness and transparency by removing any loopholes that could allow future changes which reduce supports or disadvantage disabled people without appropriate accountability or consultation.

It means promoting choice, voice, and autonomy by protecting disabled people's rights to make decisions about their own lives and who supports them, how and where. It means fostering collaboration and mutuality by partnering with disabled people, whānau, disability organizations, and Māori throughout the legislation and policy development process, now and in the future. It means supporting empowerment by ensuring you give regard to all the submissions made by disabled people and the community, and recognize their strengths, knowledge, and lived experience that we carry.

Finally, it means embracing cultural, historical, and equity responsiveness by recognizing the government's obligations to Te Tiriti o Waitangi, UNCRPD, giving regard to the outcomes from the abuse in care inquiry, addressing inequities experienced by Māori, Pacific, and other marginalized communities, and ensuring the disability support system is equitable, inclusive, and accessible for all.

So a trauma-informed Disability Support Services Act would not simply reduce harm, it would actively create the conditions for disabled people and their whānau to flourish through choice and autonomy for themselves. Thank you so much. I'm happy to take any questions if you've got any.

Questions, team? No. I'm grateful for the recommendations, though. They're very helpful. Yeah. The recommendations are great. So thank you very much for those. We will be pulling out recommendations from everybody- Yeah... as I've gone through and in making sure we cover them off as we talk through the report back stage. So thank you very much for being with us today. We appreciate your submission.
Kia ora. We are now going back to Te Taura Whakaha Mihi Nei, Mihi Nari. I know I may have said that wrong. The Anglican Disability Network.

Kia ora. Kia ora. I didn't think I will get to speak today, but yet here I participate. I'm sorry that I hadn't been able to prepare a paper for you, but I hope you can understand me. I want to say today
that we are concerned that this bill does not take into account the work that's been done in partnership with disabled people over the last 20 years. Nothing about us without us. We are all
human beings, have dignity, and we should have been asked to participate in this project. I think while I agree with this bill being around now, I agree that a legislated framework should, for

disability support services, should be put in place. And that is the only thing about this bill I agree with, because families should need to take responsibility for all its citizens. And while families are
very, very important into a lot of care. I think that we think that the state need to be the provider of disability support services, and that these support services are not means tested. And we are

also concerned about the ministerial programs that could be developed with the disability community. We need to work together and it feels like it's happening both on at, from the top down, and we know that we are
the experts in our own lives, and we need to work together to make sure that all disabled people are heard and are getting the support. We all need to fight and let life to turn more.

And that is my submission. Oh, thank you so much for your submission. It was very good. You've put a lot of thought into it. But I think it's pretty clear what you are asking of the committee to consider. So, I want to thank you very much for spending the time with us today.
Thank you. Thank you.
That was good. All right, our next submitter is Ngā Homie. Yes. Online? Yes. Great.

Okay. Ngā Homie is... Yes, here we go. Here we go. Emily. Is it Emily? Yes. Emily and Hannah-... is what I've got down here. All right. Nice to meet you. We have five minutes to hear from you. We have your submission in front of us, so please, you can take it from here.

Awesome. Kia ora koutou. So me and Emily will be doing an oral submission against this bill on behalf of Ngā Homie, which is a Rangatahi Māori-led community organization based in Kirikiriroa Hamilton. But we have members across the country. So just before I start, I whakapapa to Te Atanga Mahaki o Rongo Whakata Ngāi Tahu.

Nice. Like us. Same. As I actually have a great-grandfather who's a Kirkpatrick in Gisborne, so there's probably- Oh, fantastic... whānau connection there. So yeah, kia ora. There you go. Be careful what you wish for. And then on my father's side, I whakapapa to Scotland. And I'm a student at the University of Waikato.

I also have whānau who are support workers or work closely with people with disabilities in the health sector. So yeah. Tena koutou. Pass to Emily. Kia ora. Hi, I'm Emily, and I'm just going to kind of share my perspective as someone with a disability.

So, I oppose this bill because I believe it changes the direction of disability support in Aotearoa in ways that could have long-lasting consequences for people like me. I live with an extremely rare connective tissue genetic condition. It affects every aspect of my daily life, but it has never impacted me for what I want for my future.
